summary: This week's AI news highlights significant advancements in AI technology, including improved language models, increased adoption of AI in various industries, and growing concerns about AI's impact on society. Key developments include Google's revamped image search, Spotify's new AI-powered chat feature, and New York's moratorium on data center construction.
insight: The increasing focus on open models and customizable AI solutions suggests a shift towards more transparent and controllable AI technologies, addressing concerns about trust, ownership, and accountability.
highlights: Google's new image search uses AI to create personalized galleries based on user interests
Spotify's AI-powered chat feature allows users to discover music and podcasts through conversational interactions
New York's data center moratorium highlights growing concerns about the environmental impact of AI infrastructure

articles.title: Google revamps image search for its 25th anniversary with more images and more AI
articles.description: The new Google image search will use your "unique interests" to create an always-updated gallery.

articles.title: Google Images gets a Pinterest-like redesign focused on discovery
articles.description: Now, when users navigate to Google Images, they'll see a "For You" gallery of images tailored to their interests and browsing history.

articles.title: Reflection inks $1B compute deal with Nebius
articles.description: Reflection AI has signed a $1 billion deal to access Nebius' compute. Reflection was founded in 2024 and is developing open source AI technology.

articles.title: Spotify expands its AI push with a ChatGPT-like music assistant
articles.description: Spotify is rolling out a new AI-powered conversational feature that lets Premium subscribers chat with the app to discover music, podcasts, audiobooks, and more.

articles.title: How sales teams use ChatGPT Work
articles.description: See how sales teams can use ChatGPT Work to create pipeline briefs, meeting prep packets, forecast reviews, account plans, and stalled-deal diagnoses from real work inputs.
